reasoning: TPC-H SF10 workload dominated by 60M-row lineitem scans across three distinct patterns. Q1 and Q6 already achieve competitive performance; Q3's 3-way join (customer→orders→lineitem) is 4.2x slower than DuckDB (386ms vs 91ms). Critical optimizations: (1) Exploit 64-core parallelism with morsel-driven execution; (2) Fix Q3's join materialization bottleneck through optimal join ordering and radix partitioning; (3) Leverage existing storage design (zone maps, sorted indexes, dictionary encoding) with aggressive filter pushdown. Hardware supports AVX512 vectorization for filtering and aggregation.

focus_areas: Parallelize 60M-row lineitem scan with morsel-driven execution (64 cores, ~1M rows/morsel)
Use zone map on l_shipdate for predicate pushdown—filter selectivity is 98.8% (high), apply before materialization
Thread-local hash aggregation with 6 groups (l_returnflag × l_linestatus) + single-threaded merge at end
Dictionary-decode l_returnflag/l_linestatus once before aggregation loop
Vectorize sum/avg/count operations with AVX512 (process 4-8 rows per SIMD instruction)
Fuse scan+filter+aggregation into single loop to avoid materialization; operator fusion
Final sort on 6 groups is negligible—output in GROUP BY order
Q1.strategy_notes: Q1 is a bulk scan + aggregation. Current 48ms is competitive, but parallelism unlocks further gains. Use morsel-driven execution with thread-local hash tables to minimize contention. Apply zone map filtering at scan time. Dictionary encoding on low-cardinality columns is already in storage design—leverage it. Target: 30-40ms with aggressive vectorization and parallelism.

focus_areas: Fix join ordering: apply highest-selectivity filter FIRST (c_mktsegment = 'BUILDING', 22% selectivity) to minimize intermediate rows

Two-phase strategy: (1) filter customer on mktsegment (22% → ~330K rows), (2) join with filtered orders (o_orderdate < '1995-03-15', 33% selectivity on unfiltered → ~5M orders matched to ~330K customers)
For the 3-way join (customer→orders→lineitem): build hash table on filtered customer.c_custkey (330K rows, fits in L3 cache), probe with filtered orders, then probe result with lineitem on l_orderkey
Use radix partitioning on l_orderkey if join intermediate exceeds memory budget (partition into 64 subpartitions, each ~1M rows)
Hash index on c_mktsegment (already in storage design) enables efficient customer filtering
Sorted index on o_custkey (already in storage design) enables efficient customer→orders join
Sorted index on l_orderkey (already in storage design) enables efficient orders→lineitem join
Parallelize filtered scans with morsel-driven execution (64 cores)
Final aggregation: group by (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority) → ~100K groups; use hash aggregation with partial agg per thread
TOP-K optimization: use heap sort on 10 rows (sort_topk), not full sort on 100K rows
Vectorize predicates (date/quantity comparisons) with AVX512
Q3.strategy_notes: Q3 is the critical bottleneck (386ms vs 91ms DuckDB). Root cause: multi-way join materialization and suboptimal join ordering. Apply filters (mktsegment, orderdate) BEFORE building join hash tables to reduce intermediate cardinality. Use existing indexes on c_mktsegment, o_custkey, l_orderkey aggressively. Parallelize filtered scans. For the final step (aggregate + TOP-K), use hash aggregation with thread-local tables, then heap sort on 10 rows. Target: <150ms (competitive with DuckDB).

focus_areas: Single-table scan on lineitem with compound range predicates (selectivity: 13.4% final)
Predicate ordering: apply most selective filter first (l_discount BETWEEN 0.05-0.07, 13% selectivity), then l_quantity < 24 (48% selectivity), then l_shipdate range (16% selectivity)
Use zone map on l_shipdate to skip blocks where date range doesn't match (min/max per 128K-row block)
Vectorize predicates with AVX512: process 8 rows per instruction for date/discount/quantity comparisons
Branch-free filtering: use masked comparisons to avoid branch misprediction on tight filter loop
Morsel-driven parallel scan (64 cores, ~1M rows/morsel) with thread-local partial results (single SUM aggregate)
In-place aggregation: no GROUP BY (single result row). Accumulate SUM locally per thread, merge at end with single atomic add or final single-threaded sum
Delta encoding on l_shipdate (already in storage) reduces memory bandwidth; decode in vectorized loop
Q6.strategy_notes: Q6 is already fast (29.3ms), but parallelism can close the gap to DuckDB (20ms). Focus on vectorized filtering with AVX512 and morsel-driven parallel scan. Zone map filtering on l_shipdate eliminates unmatched blocks. Aggregate is trivial (single SUM); the bottleneck is filtering throughput. Target: 20ms with aggressive vectorization and parallelism.
